document_title: Enterprise Compliance & Regulatory Master Policies Manual (Fictional Demo)
document_note: Fictional compliance policy manual created for software demos, AI research, and compliance checks. Does not represent any real company or regulation.

policies.title: EPA Emissions Reporting Regulation
policies.owner: Director of Environmental Health and Safety
policies.business_objective: Ensure accurate corporate carbon emission reporting and prevent evasion via offshoring.
policies.policy_statement: Corporations must report 100% of Scope 1 and Scope 2 carbon emissions annually. Compute workloads, manufacturing, or operations must not be offshored to foreign partners or unregulated entities for the purpose of evading domestic reporting limits or carbon taxes.

detailed_requirements.requirement_text: Emissions must be fully reported annually for all domestic Scope 1 (direct) and Scope 2 (indirect energy) activities.
Compute workloads and operational activities must not be offshored to evade domestic reporting thresholds or carbon tax limits.

indicators_of_non_compliance: Routing high-compute jobs to offshore subcontractors to avoid domestic emissions tracking
Failing to document Scope 2 emissions from third-party server hosting facilities
Missing carbon reporting documentation for regional facilities
required_evidence: Annual carbon accounting and emissions reports
Cloud hosting routing and configuration data
Subcontractor agreements for offshore compute workloads
policies.recommended_remediation: Audit and incorporate all offshore compute activities into Scope 3 emissions calculations, adjust domestic compute loads, and establish strict tracking of all outsourced energy footprints.
policies.ai_summary: This policy mandates comprehensive annual reporting of Scope 1 and Scope 2 carbon emissions and strictly prohibits offshoring or subcontracting high-compute activities to evade domestic reporting limits or carbon tax liabilities.
policies.example_compliant_scenario: The company conducts an annual audit, reports all Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions including cloud compute usage, and registers all external vendor emissions under Scope 3.
policies.example_non_compliant_scenario: The company routes AI training models to an overseas data center in an unregulated market to artificially reduce reported domestic carbon usage and avoid carbon offset purchases.

policies.title: Remote Employee Tax Nexus Law
policies.business_objective: Ensure accurate state payroll income tax withholding based on remote employee physical work location.
policies.policy_statement: Employers must adjust state payroll tax withholdings to match the remote employee's actual physical working location if they reside and work in a different state for more than 30 consecutive days. The company must implement active physical location check-ins or automated logs to track remote work state changes.

detailed_requirements.requirement_text: State income tax withholding must accurately match the employee's physical work location.
The company must actively track physical presence and trigger nexus payroll tax adjustments when remote work in another state exceeds 30 consecutive days.

indicators_of_non_compliance: Remote employees working in a different state for >30 consecutive days under old tax codes
Absence of check-in mechanism or IP geolocation logs to verify remote work locations
Stale addresses in payroll system for known out-of-state remote workers
policies.recommended_remediation: Implement automated IP geolocation tracking for employees accessing systems and configure automated notifications to alert HR when an employee reaches 20 remote work days in a new state.
policies.ai_summary: This policy requires the tracking and adjusting of payroll tax withholding to align with the actual physical working state of remote employees who work out-of-state for more than 30 consecutive days.
policies.example_compliant_scenario: An employee moves to a new state, and the HR tracking system automatically triggers a payroll tax update to the new state after 30 days of remote IP logins.
policies.example_non_compliant_scenario: An employee works from a summer home in another state for three months, but payroll taxes continue to be withheld under their primary residence state due to lack of tracking.

policies.title: Customer Data Privacy & Opt-in Consent
policies.business_objective: Protect consumer data from unauthorized monetization and prevent coercive consent practices.
policies.policy_statement: Explicit, affirmative opt-in consent must be obtained from users before selling or sharing their personal data with external advertisers. Core services and software features must remain fully functional and performant even if the user declines consent.

detailed_requirements.requirement_text: Explicit opt-in consent must be gathered prior to customer data monetization or sharing with advertising networks.
Access to core features, performance, and services must not be degraded, restricted, or charged extra if data monetization consent is refused.

indicators_of_non_compliance: User data transmitted to third-party ad networks without an explicit opt-in event
Core app functionality blocked or slow-tracked for users who opt out of monetization
Use of pre-checked check boxes or deceptive dark patterns to coerce consent
required_evidence: Signup flow screenshots and design documents
Network telemetry and ad SDK request logs
Feature flag and user access matrices
policies.recommended_remediation: Implement a data privacy boundary in the API that blocks third-party SDK calls for users unless an explicit opt_in flag is set to true.
policies.ai_summary: This policy mandates obtaining explicit user consent prior to data monetization and forbids degrading or restricting core services for users who opt out.
policies.example_compliant_scenario: A new customer signs up and checks 'No' to sharing data for ads; they access the entire portal at normal speed without data sharing.
policies.example_non_compliant_scenario: A user who opts out of data sharing finds that the load time of the dashboard is artificially throttled to 10 seconds to force them to opt in.

policies.title: Material Cybersecurity Incident Disclosure Act
policies.business_objective: Ensure public and regulatory bodies are promptly informed of material cyber risks and incidents.
policies.policy_statement: Publicly reportable cybersecurity incidents determined to be material to the organization's business, finances, or operations must be disclosed to the appropriate regulatory body within four (4) business days of the materiality determination. Materiality determinations must be formally documented and co-signed by the CISO and CFO.

detailed_requirements.requirement_text: Material cybersecurity incidents must be disclosed to regulators within four (4) business days of the materiality determination.
Materiality determinations must be formally documented and co-signed by both the CISO and the CFO.

indicators_of_non_compliance: Regulatory filings for cyber breaches filed more than 4 business days after internal discovery and materiality determination
Lack of documented materiality assessments or missing executive signatures
Unstructured, verbal-only materiality evaluations with no audit trail
policies.recommended_remediation: Establish a formal breach assessment protocol with SLA alerts for executive review and sign-off templates.
policies.ai_summary: This policy mandates public disclosure of material cyber incidents within 4 business days of determining materiality and requires signed executive documentation.
policies.example_compliant_scenario: The security operations center detects a data breach, evaluates the business impact, files a signed materiality report on day 2, and submits a regulatory disclosure on day 3.
policies.example_non_compliant_scenario: The company discovers an active database breach but keeps it quiet for 30 days during a forensic audit without documenting a formal materiality decision.

policies.title: CFPB Predatory Lending Cap
policies.business_objective: Protect consumers from predatory interest rates and hidden borrowing fees.
policies.policy_statement: Consumer micro-loans and credit products must not exceed an effective Annual Percentage Rate (APR) of no more than 36%. The APR calculation must be fully inclusive of all interest rates, processing fees, mandatory subscription models, account charges, and platform access costs.

detailed_requirements.requirement_text: The effective Annual Percentage Rate (APR) on all consumer loans and micro-loans must not exceed 36%.
The APR calculation must include all mandatory fees, subscriptions, and platform access charges required to obtain the loan.

indicators_of_non_compliance: Loans marketed at 35% nominal APR but requiring a mandatory monthly platform subscription that pushes the effective rate above 36%
Loan calculators that omit mandatory platform fees from the APR disclosure
Charging mandatory administrative activation fees that are kept separate from the reported APR calculation
required_evidence: APR calculation algorithms and system config
policies.recommended_remediation: Audit the pricing model to integrate all compulsory user fees into the APR calculation, updating the loan contract template to show the correct APR.
policies.ai_summary: This policy caps the effective APR for consumer loans at 36% and requires the calculation to encompass all mandatory fees and platform subscriptions.
policies.example_compliant_scenario: A loan has a 20% interest rate and a mandatory $2 fee, and the loan calculator combines both into a declared APR of 22%.
policies.example_non_compliant_scenario: A loan is offered at 29% interest but forces the user to buy a $10/month 'account care' subscription, bringing the effective APR to 45% without disclosing it.
